coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using glycerol and citric acid under microwave radiation, achieving an 83 % quantum yield (QY) and 1.59 nm particle size. These CQDs were employed as softeners and crosslinkers for the production of fluorescent granular and film hydrogels, in combination with biopolymers such as starch, chitosan (Ch), and gelatin (Ge). The resulting biopolymer-CQD composites were evaluated for their ability to selectively detect heavy metal ions, such as iron (III), as well as for their water vapor permeability (WVP), water solubility (WS), and swelling properties. Various analyses confirmed the formation of fluorescence bio-nanocomposites and improvements in their properties. Notably, a hydrogel film containing 50% wt CQDs exhibited the highest mechanical strength, with a tensile strength of 6.28 MPa and an elongation at fracture of 91.2%. Additionally, a strongly crosslinked fluorescent granular hydrogel with a ratio of Ge 25%: Ch 75% and a fixed amount of CQDs was able to absorb 136 times its own weight in water and exhibited increased water vapor permeability with higher CQD content. Overall, the fabricated materials show potential for the detection of iron (II), iron (III), and mercury (II) in aqueous media.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent biopolymer hydrogels crosslinked with carbon quantum dots for selective detection of heavy iron (III) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Polymer, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Polymer, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using glycerol and citric acid under microwave radiation, achieving an 83 % quantum yield (QY) and 1.59 nm particle size. These CQDs were employed as softeners and crosslinkers for the production of fluorescent granular and film hydrogels, in combination with biopolymers such as starch, chitosan (Ch), and gelatin (Ge). The resulting biopolymer-CQD composites were evaluated for their ability to selectively detect heavy metal ions, such as iron (III), as well as for their water vapor permeability (WVP), water solubility (WS), and swelling properties. Various analyses confirmed the formation of fluorescence bio-nanocomposites and improvements in their properties. Notably, a hydrogel film containing 50% wt CQDs exhibited the highest mechanical strength, with a tensile strength of 6.28 MPa and an elongation at fracture of 91.2%. Additionally, a strongly crosslinked fluorescent granular hydrogel with a ratio of Ge 25%: Ch 75% and a fixed amount of CQDs was able to absorb 136 times its own weight in water and exhibited increased water vapor permeability with higher CQD content. Overall, the fabricated materials show potential for the detection of iron (II), iron (III), and mercury (II) in aqueous media.

ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ is a vital trace element essential for human health, playing a crucial role in biological systems as a necessary cofactor in various proteins. The presence of too little or too much iron (III) ions in a living organism can result in a range of disorders related to the transport, storage, and equilibrium of iron, which are tightly regulated. This can lead to various health hazards, including anemia, pathological conditions, and skin diseases [1]. Especially, ferric iron, Fe (III), is primarily stored within cells, either in its unbound state or bound to proteins, serving structural or catalytic functions [2]. Additionally, Fe3+ is a major industrial process pollutant constantly released into the environment, causing significant environmental issues and becoming a focus in natural water and oceanography studies [3]. Consequently, the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ holds great significance for biological and environmental systems. Various methods have been developed for determining Fe3+, including colorimetry, electrochemistry, absorption, and capillary electrophoresis [4]. The fluorescence method offers distinct advantages such as high sensitivity, good reproducibility, minimal sample requirement, and rapid analysis [5].

Lately, there has been a rapid increase in the development of natural polymer-based edible films and coatings due to environmental concerns associated with petroleum-based plastics [6]. Researchers are exploring new biodegradable materials in response to the growing non-organic waste sources and challenges in recycling synthetic plastics [7]. Starch is considered a highly promising raw material for these applications due to its biodegradability, abundance, renewability, low cost, and processability [8]. Starch-based films share similar physical characteristics with synthetic polymers, such as being tasteless, transparent, and odorless, making them suitable for various plastic applications [9,10]. However, thermoplastic starch (TPS) films, while naturally renewable and cost-effective, are hydrophilic, brittle, have high humidity barrier properties, and low mechanical strength compared to synthetic plastic films [11,12]. Additionally, TPS films undergo recrystallization or retrogradation during storage, impacting their functional properties [13]. To address these limitations, efforts have focused on combining natural starch with synthetic polymers, fillers, and reinforcement substances in TPS production processes involving mechanical and thermal energies in the presence of plasticizers on starch granules [14,15].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are highly appealing among various nanocarbons due to their ability to absorb visible light and their remarkable physicochemical properties [16,17]. These zero-dimensional carbonaceous nanomaterials, typically less than 10 nm in size, present a compelling option as semiconductor photocatalyst alternatives. They exhibit strong photo responsiveness, redox properties, stability, nontoxic characteristics, and easy tunability [18]. CQDs are characterized by a diverse distribution of sp2 and sp3 carbon domains with heteroatomic functional groups, resulting in discrete energy levels and unique photophysical, chemical, and electrical properties [19]. Their electron-accepting, electron-transfer, and electron-transport properties make them promise for light harvesting. With high aqueous solubility, ease of synthesis and modification, and edge effects, CQDs are widely utilized in photo-mediated applications and sensing technologies, including biomarker sensing, detection of heavy metal ions, DNA, biomolecules, hydrogen generation, CO2 reduction, and dye degradation [20–22]. CQDs also can be used in biological activities. For example, in a particular investigation, Ahmed et al. [23] employed CQDs for their potential as antitumor and anti-inflammatory agents, utilizing the Griess assay. In their other study, they utilized polymer dots as a biomedicine [24]. Carbon nanodots in one study were employed as viricidal and anticancer laborers by Emam et al [25]. CQDS exhibited excellent antimicrobial potency in one study by Ahmed et al [26]. While CQDs have been used as additives for photocatalysts due to their electron-transfer and light-harvesting properties, their limited photocatalytic activity under visible light has hindered their exploration for visible-light-induced photocatalysis [27]. The detection of Fe3+ typically relies on dyes, which have poor absorbing ability and can cause photobleaching and instability [28]. Various nanomaterial fluorescent probes, such as graphene QDs, metallic hydroxides, carbon nanodots, and metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), have been reported for detecting Fe3+ [18,29]. The performance of these probes in Fe3+ determination is significantly influenced by their surface features. Challenges include cross-sensitivity to other metal ions and poor sensitivity with good accuracy. Therefore, developing suitable fluorescent nanoprobes and gaining a deep understanding of the mechanism for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ are essential for biological and environmental analysis applications [30].

Numerous researchers have investigated the development of fluorescence hydrogels for detecting various ions. For example, Li et al. [31] developed bright blue, fluorescent N-MQDs from Ta4C3 as the precursor for detecting Fe3+, achieving a high quantum yield (QY) of 23.4 % and excellent fluorescence stability. Zhou et al. [32] explored a radiometric fluorescence sensor based on CdTe quantum dots (QDs) doped hydrogel optical fiber for real-time selective detection of Fe3+ ions, achieving a detection limit of 14 nM in a linear range of 0–3.5 μM. Guo et al. [33] investigated Hg2+ ion detection using carbon dots incorporated in nanocomposite hydrogels, achieving a low detection limit of 4 nM and a linear range from 0 to 5 μM. Zhang et al [34]. prepared a PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel with stable fluorescence detection for Fe3+, demonstrating a low detection limit of 0.27 μM. Fan et al. [35] assessed a chitosan-based fluorescent hydrogel for selective detection of Fe2+ ions, achieving detection limits of 0.28 ppm in gel-to-sol mode and 3.8 ppb in turn-off fluorescence mode. Deng et al. [36] studied the preparation of a chitosan–sulfur quantum dots (CS-SQDs) composite hydrogel with excellent sensing and uptake capability toward Cr (VI) ions, reaching a detection limit of 176.2 nM. Maity et al. [37] developed a fluorescence hydrogel using chitosan, polyvinyl alcohol, and 9-anthraldehyde (ChPA). This hydrogel displays fluorescence in both gel and solution phases. When exposed to Fe3+ ions at a concentration of 0.124 nM, the fluorescence intensity increased approximately 3.45 times, while in the presence of Fe2+ ions, the fluorescence intensity decreased by 2.92 times. Wu et al [38]. developed a new composite material consisting of fluorescent CQDs and hydrogel, which exhibited effective adsorption and stable fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ions. The study demonstrated that the combined use of CQDs and hydrogel improved their synergistic performance for adsorption and quantitative detection of heavy metal ions in water. Khattab et al [39]. utilized a sensor based on chitosan nanoparticles (CsNPs) as a biopolymer host and cyanidin phenol chromophore as a biomolecular probe. The Cy-CsNPs-CPS sensor displayed high selectivity for ferric ions, with no color change observed for other metal cations. In another study, Cheng et al. [40] prepared a smart fluorescent PEI-CDs/hydrogel nanocomposite with Fe3+ sensing capability using PEI-CDs as the luminophore for Fe3+ ion detection. Compared to using only PEI-CDs for Fe3+ ion detection, the smart hydrogel nanocomposite significantly improved the photo-stability of PEI-CDs by preventing self-quenching. Cayuela et al [41]. conducted a study on the direct determination of silver ions (Ag+) using luminescent Carbon Quantum Dot hydrogels (CQDGs). The use of these gels led to considerable fluorescence enhancement and significantly influenced selectivity. Khattab et al [2]. also developed a solid-state sensor for the determination of ferric ions (Fe3+) in aqueous media. The sensor demonstrated high selectivity for Fe3+ ions within a pH range from 3.2 to 8.8, with no color change observed for other metals.

Our study introduces an innovative method for producing eco-friendly and cost-efficient fluorescent probes to detect metal ions. While previous research has focused on using fluorescent probes to detect various ions, our study is the first to employ green chemistry principles in creating CQDs for this purpose. By utilizing only glycerin and citric acid, our approach eliminates the need for toxic chemicals and reduces processing time, offering a more sustainable alternative compared to traditional quantum dot synthesis methods. Additionally, we have successfully integrated CQDs into films and hydrogels, demonstrating exceptional properties such as high-water vapor permeability, water solubility, and swelling. These materials hold potential for applications in sensing and bioimaging. Furthermore, our fluorescent probes display strong affinity and specificity towards target metal ions, including iron (II), iron (III), and mercury (II), making them promising candidates for detecting these ions in environmental and biomedical samples. In summary, our study represents a significant advancement in developing functional materials using green chemistry principles, offering a more sustainable and cost-effective approach for synthesizing fluorescent probes with potential applications across various fields. The most important novelty of this work is the synthesis of CQDs in this study. In this study, glycerol and citric acid were used in the fabrication of CQDs for the first time. It is notable that these CQDs were used as a softener and crosslinker in fluorescent films.

In this investigation, CQDs were utilized as effective probes for detecting metal cations, specifically iron (III). The study involved integrating CQDs with natural, biocompatible, and biodegradable biopolymers such as starch, chitosan, and gelatin to create samples for examining the morphology of fluorescent hydrogel films. These samples were evaluated for their selectivity in detecting heavy iron ions (III) through a straightforward and rapid method.

Corn starch ((C6H12O6)n), Chitosan (C12H24N2O9), Gelatin (C12H24N2O4), Glycerol (C3H8O3), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Citric acid (C6H8O7), Acetic acid (CH3COOH), Formaldehyde (CH2O), Calcium sulfate (CaSO4), Potassium sulfate (K2SO4), Aluminum sulfate (Al2(So4)3), Iron (II) sulfate (FeSO4), Iron (III) sulfate (Fe2(So4)3), Lead (II) sulfate (PbSo4), Nickel (II) sulfate (NiSO4), Mercury (II) sulfate (HgSO4), Cobalt (II) sulfate (CoSO4), Manganese (II) sulfate (Mn SO4), Zinc sulfate (ZnSO4) were commercially purchased from Merck, Germany.

A Kern model, ABJ 220-4NM digital scale was used to weigh the materials. A Fan AzmaGostar magnetic stirrer from Tehran, Iran was used to prevent the agglomeration of solutions. Specimens were obtained using a BINDER ED 23 heating oven from Germany. The microwave-assisted carbonization reaction was carried out in a Panasonic NN-SD797S genius microwave oven from Japan. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) using a Bruker Tensor 27 instrument from Germany was used to characterize functional groups. The morphology and size of samples were assessed using a Tescan MIRA3FEG-SEM scanning electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a Bruker AG Siemens D500 instrument from Germany was used to determine the size and crystallite structure of samples. Photoluminescence (PL) was performed using a JASCO FP-6200 instrument from China to demonstrate the presence of CQDs in the films. UV–visible absorption spectra were employed to demonstrate the adsorption and emission of samples using a Pharmacia Biotech Ultrospec 2000 spectrometer from the United Kingdom. The stability (zeta potential) and size distribution of the samples were characterized using a Malvern model Nanotrac Wave (Microtrace) dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument with a 658 nm diode laser. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was conducted using a JADE DSC instrument from Perkin Elmer, USA, calibrated by indium and silver. An empty aluminum container was used as a reference, and the thermal behavior of the samples, weighing approximately 5 mg, started from a temperature of 30 °C and continued at a constant rate of 5 °C min–1 to 300 °C.

CQDs based on glycerol and citric acid were synthesized by green and microwave method in such a way that first 1 gr of citric acid was dissolved in 4.5 ml of glycerol for 24 h with gentle stirring in order to modify the surface and increase the quantum efficiency. The microwave was placed for 10 min with a power of 600 W. Finally, the obtained product is washed 3 times with ethyl acetate and kept in the freezer for half an hour. Excess ethyl acetate is separated from the viscous solution by overflowing the CQDs, and the viscous solution is placed in an oven at 40 °C for three days to evaporate the excess ethyl acetate and prepare not pure CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the thermoplastic film based on starch (TPS)
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare TPS films, the casting method was used. First, 2.5 g of starch was dissolved in 50 ml of distilled water at a temperature of 75 °C and stirred at a speed of 150 rpm. Then, 50 % wt. Starch (1.25 g) and glycerin and citric acid (1.25 g) were added to the solution and stirred by heating for half an hour. The resulting solution was poured into a polystyrene petri dish and placed in an oven at a temperature of 55 °C. After 20 h, the film was separated from the petri dish as a flexible film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent films containing starch-based carbon quantum dots (TPS-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, starch-based fluorescent films were prepared by casting method. First, 2.5 gr of starch was dissolved in 50 ml of distilled water at a temperature of 75 °C and stirred at a speed of 150 rpm, and then 1.25 gr of raw CQDs (unpurified) were added to the solution and stirred for half an hour by heating. The resulting solution was poured into a polystyrene petri dish and placed in an oven at a temperature of 55 °C. After 20 h, the film was separated from the petri dish as a flexible film. This synthesis was done with different weight ratios of starch and CQDs and the ratios reported in this synthesis are optimal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent granular hydrogels containing carbon quantum dots based on chitosan
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare chitosan hydrogels beads (Ch-CQDs), first, 0.1 g of chitosan was dissolved in 6 cc of 1 % acetic acid solution with gentle magnetic stirring. Then, 300 µl of raw (unpurified) CQDs were added to the chitosan solution and stirred for three hours. Next, with a syringe, the resulting solution was added dropwise into a beaker containing 2.5 M sodium hydroxide solution placed in an ice bath. This caused physical ionic cross-linking, forming hydrogel beads. The beads were mixed gently with a magnet to prevent them from sticking together. The hydrogel beads were then washed several times with distilled water to remove any residual NaOH. Subsequently, the beads were soaked in formaldehyde solution for 90 min to induce chemical cross-linking. Finally, the cross-linked hydrogel beads were freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent granular hydrogels containing carbon quantum dots based on chitosan–gelatin
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare chitosan–gelatin hydrogel beads (Ch: Ge-CQDs), first, chitosan and gelatin were dissolved in the ratios of 1:3 and 1:1 in 6 mL of 1% acetic acid solution. The mixture was gently stirred with a magnet, and then 300 μL of carbon quantum dots were added and stirred for three hours. Next, with a syringe, the resulting solution was added dropwise into a beaker containing 2.5 M sodium hydroxide solution placed in an ice bath. This caused physical ionic cross-linking, forming hydrogel beads. The beads were mixed gently with a magnet to prevent them from sticking together. The hydrogel beads were then washed several times with distilled water to remove any residual NaOH. Subsequently, the beads are soaked in formaldehyde solution for 90 min to induce chemical cross-linking. Finally, the cross-linked hydrogel beads were freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The method of measuring the solubility of samples in water
ce:section.ce:para: Solubility in water means the percentage of dry matter of the film that becomes soluble after 24 h of immersion in distilled water. To measure the solubility, samples were first prepared in the form of small pieces and then placed in an oven at 100 °C for 24 h to reach the initial dry weight. The initial dry weight of the samples is determined, and then each piece of them is immersed in a lidded container containing distilled water and stirred from time to time, and then the samples are taken out at different times and placed in an oven at 100 °C for 24 h. Until it reaches a constant weight, the final dry weight is obtained by redistributing the samples.

The water solubility (WS) of the films were done according to the method described by Salari et al. [42], with some modifications. ‘WS’ indicated is the amount of water-soluble dry mass that gets dissolved in water after being immersed for a definite duration. The
W S
of the films was calculated using the Equation (1):
(1)
WS ( % ) = ( Δ M / M ) × 100
Where
Δ M
is weight loss in the water,
M
is the initial weight before immersing in water [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The method of measuring the amount of swelling of samples
ce:section.ce:para: To measure the degree of swelling of the samples, the samples were immersed in a solution with acidic, neutral and basic pHs for 24 h, and then the surface solution of the samples was dried with filter paper, and the weight of the swollen sample was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The method of measuring the permeability of film samples to water vapor
ce:section.ce:para: The water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) of the films was measured using the gravimetric method. In order to measure the water vapor permeability of the film samples, vials with a diameter of 1.5 cm and a height of 4.5 cm were used. 3 g of anhydrous calcium sulfate (relative humidity 0%) were poured into the vials and a piece of the film sample was placed over the mouth of the vial. The vials, with their contents, were weighed and then placed in a desiccator containing a saturated solution of potassium sulfate (relative humidity above 97%). The weight of the vials was measured at regular intervals over time.

The amount of tensile strength and tensile modulus of the films were measured by Universal tensile testing machine at the pharmaceutical research center. The model of this device is STM20,SANTAM, Tehran, Iran. For this test, samples with dimensions of 20 × 15 square mm were cut from the film and the test was performed with a stretching speed of 25 mm/min and at ambient temperature.

Various solutions of 500 μM concentration were prepared using the sulfate salts of iron (III), iron (II), aluminum (III), lead (II), nickel (II), mercury (II), cobalt (II), manganese (II), and zinc (II). Small pieces of the film and hydrogel beads were immersed in 15 cc of each solution, and after 20 min under UV light, the fluorescent properties of the films were observed. The fluorescence of the film placed in the iron (III) sulfate solution was steadily reduced and almost extinguishes after 20 min, while the fluorescent properties were maintained in the samples exposed to the other metal sulfate solutions.

The FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs obtained via the microwave method was recorded both before and after the washing and purification processes, as depicted in Fig. 1 . It is evident that a broad band in the range of 3600–3100 cm−1 was attributed to the O–H stretching vibration [16,44]. Furthermore, the observed IR band in this range was linked to the CQDs of citric acid and glycerol, signifying the presence of the hydroxyl (R-OH) group in the structure of citric acid and glycerol [45]. Consequently, the overlap of water and acidic vibrations resulted in a wider band in this region. The faint band at approximately 2916 cm−1 was associated with the C–H stretching bond. Additionally, the band at 1733 cm−1 was assigned to the carbonyl groups (C = O bond) on the carbon quantum dots [21,46]. The prominent bands at 1460 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 represented the symmetric and anti-symmetric vibrations of the (CO2 –) group, respectively, consistent with the presence of the acidic group on the surface of the carbon quantum dots [48,70]. A comparison of the two FT-IR spectra before and after the washing process indicates that the washing and purification process could influence the intensity of FT-IR bands. It is apparent that after the purification of CQDs, the intensity of bands gradually decreased, suggesting that the washing process may have removed some of the functional groups present on the surface of the carbon quantum dots, leading to a decrease in the intensity of specific FT-IR bands associated with those functional groups.

Fig. 2
a displays the FT-IR spectra of TPS-CQDs, revealing asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of various hydroxyl groups in the range of 3800––3600 cm−1 [49,50]. Two bands at 1692 and 1742 cm−1 were attributed to the carbonyl groups (C = O bond) on the carbon quantum dots and starch carboxyl and ester groups. A minor band in the region of 1651 cm−1 is associated with the O–H bending group of water absorbed by starch in the film. Additionally, the bands in the range of 1400–1520 cm−1 are linked to the bending vibrations and deformation of carbon and hydrogen atoms, while those at 1267 and 955 cm−1 are related to the stretching C-O group [48,51,52,71]. In Fig. 2 b, the FT-IR spectra of Ch-CQDs granule hydrogel are depicted. A broad band at 3400 cm−1 was attributed to the O–H and N–H stretching vibration, while the band at 2925 cm−1 was assigned to the C–H stretching bond [53]. The amide I (C = O stretching) band in the chitosan structure appeared in the 1601 cm−1 region. The sharp and long band at 1364 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibrations of C-N existing in amide III, and the peak at 1062 cm−1 represents the C-O-C ether group. The –OH and N–H bond observed at 3400 cm−1 was broadened by the formation of intermolecular H bonds between glycerol, water, and chitosan [54]. Fig. 2 c presents the FT-IR spectra of Ch: Ge-CQDs hydrogel after gelatin addition. The intensity of the band at 3400 cm−1 increased, indicating the presence of amino groups. The intensity of all bands steadily increased, providing evidence of the presence of gelatin in the structure of this hydrogel [20].

The surface morphology was examined at various magnifications, as depicted in Fig. 3 . The impact of adding CQDs on the surface structure of TPS-CQDs composite films was investigated using SEM. In Fig. 3 a, SEM images of the thermoplastic starch film containing carbon quantum dots (TPS-CQDs) with a ratio of 1.25 g CQDs to 2.5 g starch are shown at magnifications of 10 µm and 20 µm. In Fig. 3 b, SEM images of fluorescent chitosan granular hydrogel with carbon quantum dots (Ch-CQDs) at different magnifications (500 nm and 200 nm). The surface morphology is spongy and hollow, indicating a porous structure with a soft surface. Fig. 3 c displays SEM images of Ch: Ge-CQDs with a ratio of Ge 25 % to Ch 75 % at magnifications of 500 nm and 200 nm, showing a porous structure with a spongy and hollow morphology. This hydrogel is more compact than chitosan hydrogel with carbon quantum dots (Ch-CQDs) due to its strong crosslinks.

The utilization of x-rays for material analysis enables the detailed determination of information regarding the system's order and disorder state [55,56,72,73]. The structure of starch is affected by the plant it originates from, leading to various x-ray diffraction patterns in starch granules. These patterns include A-type, commonly found in cereals, B-type, more prevalent in starches with high amylose content, and C-type, which combines A and B patterns [57]. X-ray diffraction of corn starch revealed A-type peaks. The XRD spectrum of TPS-CQDs film in the 2θ = 10-80° region (depicted in Fig. 4
a). The main peaks at 2θ = 17.5° and 20.5°, and relatively wide and small peaks at 34.5°, all associated with starch polymer matrix. The films' x-ray diffraction pattern, compared to other studies on corn starch, indicated amorphousness due to CQDs breaking hydrogen bonds within the starch molecule, softening it, and disrupting its granular structure [58]. In Fig. 4 b, the x-ray diffraction of Ch-CQDs displayed a broad peak at 2θ = 20° and small peaks at 2θ = 30, 32, and 40 degrees, related to polymer network matrix, suggesting hydrogel's amorphousness or its low crystallinity. The interconnected structure allows the glucosamine units to play a more significant role in the polysaccharide sequence, reducing the strength of the hydrogen bonds in the new compound and lowering its crystallinity [59]. Moving on to Fig. 4 c, it illustrates the x-ray diffraction of Ch: Ge-CQDs, showing a broad peak at 20° and a small peak at 30°, both associated with the polymer network matrix. The width of these peaks indicates the hydrogel's low crystallinity or amorphous nature, which is influenced by the presence of gelatin in the composition, differing from the Ch-CQDs sample [60].

Following the observation of CQDs fluorescence under a UV lamp emitting blue light, the specimens are prepared for absorption and emission spectra. The absorption and emission spectrum of CQDs is depicted in Fig. 5 . The resulting CQDs' absorption spectrum displays a peak at λmax = 295 nm, attributed to the n → π* transition of C = O, while the emission peak is linked to the π → π* transition of C = C. Additionally, the CQDs emission peak is observed at λmax = 443 nm, indicating the fluorescence properties of the synthesized nanoparticles [48].

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY), a significant parameter for fluorescent materials, was utilized to quantitatively assess the intensity of fluorescence. A concentration equal to the concentration of CQDs, with an absorbance of approximately 0.96 at 296 nm, exhibited a consistent quantum yield of 83%.

where
Φ
represents the quantum yield, the subscript “
c
” and “
s
” represents the quantum yield, respectively. I mean the integrated area under the emission spectrum; A is the absorbance at 296 nm wavelength and
η
is refractive index of the solvent. To minimize the reabsorption effect,
A C
and
A S
in the 1 cm fluorescence cuvette were kept below 0.10. In this work, the QY of TPS-CQDs in aqueous suspension was determined by a well-established reference method (quinine sulfate in 0.05 M H2SO4;
Φ S
: 54 %,
η S
: 1.33) [61,62].

The fluorescence of the prepared films under UV light is also depicted in Fig. 6 . These films were prepared using various weight ratios of starch with CQDs and without CQDs. The change in color observed in the granular films, with varying weight percentages of CQDs when exposed to UV light, is attributed to the quantum confinement effect. This effect causes CQDs to emit different colors based on their size, with smaller CQDs emitting bluer light and larger CQDs emitting redder light. As the weight percentage of CQDs decreases, the size distribution of the CQDs changes, resulting in a shift in the emitted color towards the red end of the spectrum and a paler color compared to films with higher weight percentages of CQDs [30,48].

Fig. 7 displays the UV–Vis spectra of the prepared TPS-CQDs with various weight ratios of CQDs, all with an approximate thickness of 0.36 mm. Based on the obtained spectrum, it can be inferred that the films are transparent from λmax = 300 nm to above.

Fig. 8
a, b depicts the solid-state photoluminescence spectrum of fluorescent thermoplastic films with varying concentrations of carbon quantum dots (TPS-CQDs) at two different excitation wavelengths of 300 and 350 nm. In Fig. 8 a, b, 60 wt% CQDs + 40 wt% TPS, 50 wt% CQDs + 50 wt% TPS, and 40 wt% CQDs + 60 wt% TPS films are observed. Upon analyzing this figure, it is evident that the photoluminescence spectrum of TPS-CQD nanocomposite hydrogel films is broader than the CQD spectrum in an aqueous solvent. This is likely due to phenomena such as intersystem crossing, and internal and external conversion. Additionally, the photoluminescence spectrum demonstrates the dependence of fluorescence on the concentration of nanoparticles in the polymer substrate. As the concentration of nanoparticles in the polymer matrix increases, self-absorption phenomena occur, leading to fission in the polymer substrate and an increase in fluorescence intensity [63]. The 60 wt% CQDs + 40 wt% TPS exhibited a higher intensity peak at wavelengths exceeding 800 nm, possibly due to their increased aromatic character with decorative substituents. These substituents contribute to the extension of conjugation, resulting in a noticeable increase in their fluorescence intensity [17].

Fig. 8 c, d displays the emission spectrum of fluorescent granular hydrogels with fixed amounts of CQDs and specific weight ratios of gelatin (Gl) and chitosan (Ch) at different excitation wavelengths. In Fig. 8 c, d, 100 wt% Chitosan + 300 Âµl CQDs, 75 wt% Chitosan + 25 wt% Gelatin + 300 Âµl CQDs, and 50 wt% Chitosan + 50 wt% Gelatin + 300 Âµl CQDs can be seen. The prepared samples exhibit different emissions at 307 and 340 nm due to the varying excitation wavelengths. Chitosan nanocomposite with CQDs demonstrates a higher emission intensity than gelatin-chitosan nanocomposite with CQDs, and as the amount of chitosan decreases and gelatin increases, the emission intensity decreases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DLS spectrum and zeta potential of as-fabricated CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis was conducted to determine the size distribution of CQDs. The DLS results for the obtained CQDs is depicted in Fig. 9
a, showing the size of synthesized CQDs. The smallest size is 1.57 nm, and the largest size is 1.64 nm, with the average size of having the highest percentage of abundance at 1.59 nm. Additionally, the surface charge of the CQDs was measured after dissolving them in distilled water, resulting in a charge of + 25 mV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal properties of films using DSC
ce:section.ce:para: The differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) was used to determine the transition temperature and enthalpy of the films. In the DSC heating curve of semi-crystalline polymers like starch, two types of phase transition are observed. The first-order phase transition, indicated by an endothermic peak, is related to the melting of crystalline regions. The second-order phase transition, or glass transition, is related to the transition of amorphous regions. In the heating curve of the pure starch film prepared with glycerin and citric acid, the glass transition is not clearly discernible. It is often challenging to determine the glass transition temperature of carbohydrate films containing softeners using the DSC device due to the minimal changes in heat capacity during the glass transition of these biopolymers [64].

Fig. 10
a displays the DSC curves of TPS and TPS-CQD films. The pure starch film, prepared with glycerin and citric acid, exhibits higher thermal resistance and a melting temperature of 197 °C, compared to the nanocomposite samples prepared with CQDs, where the melting temperature of the TPS-CQDs sample is 157 °C. This difference can be attributed to the presence of pure citric acid in the film's composition, acting as a cross-linker. By establishing bonds with the starch chains, it increases the order and expansion of the crystalline parts, thereby enhancing the degree of crystallinity and the melting temperature of the films. In contrast, CQDs prepared based on glycerin and citric acid are used as crosslinkers in the nanocomposite samples.

Fig. 10 b presents the DSC spectrum of fluorescent granular hydrogels containing CQDs with specific weight ratios of chitosan and gelatin. The melting temperature of the prepared samples shows minimal variation. The endothermic peak related to the melting temperature of chitosan granular hydrogel with CQDs is observed at 228 °C. Similarly, the endothermic peak corresponding to the melting temperature of chitosan–gelatin granular hydrogel with CQDs is seen at 226 °C, indicating a small difference. This difference is attributed to the presence of gelatin in the composition, which is less stable at higher temperatures and melts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examining stress–strain curves of hydrogel films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 shows the stress–strain curves of thermoplastic hydrogel films (TPS-CQDs), each graph is represented by 5 points (P, E, Y, U, F). Where P: proportional limit, E: elastic limit, Y: yield point, U: ultimate strength, F: fracture point or the breakpoint. When no CQDs are added, the fracture point is at 10. With 60 wt% CQDs added, the fracture point shifts to 30. With 50 wt% CQDs added, the fracture point is at 27. When 40 wt% CQDs are added, the fracture point is at 20. The effect of CQDs on the strain can be observed by analyzing these fracture points. As the percentage of CQDs added increases, the fracture point tends to shift to higher values. This indicates that the addition of CQDs affects the material's ability to withstand stress before fracturing. The increase in fracture point with higher CQD concentrations suggests that CQDs may enhance the material's mechanical properties, leading to improved strain resistance.

Fig. 12 illustrates the strain to fracture (a) and tensile strength (b) of TPS hydrogel films with different concentrations of CQDs and without CQDs. It can be observed that the hydrogel film with 50 % wt of glycerol and citric acid has a tensile strength of 3.085 MPa and a strain to fracture of 61.03 %, while the hydrogel film with 50 % by weight of CQDs exhibits a tensile strength of 6.28 MPa and a strain to fracture of 91.2 %. Both the tensile strength and the percentage of elongation have improved, indicating the proper distribution of CQDs in the polymer matrix. Additionally, the hydrogel film with 40 % by weight of CQDs displays a tensile strength of 14.27 MPa and a strain to fracture of 28.66 percent, suggesting that the resulting film is hard, and brittle compared to the film with 50 % weight of CQDs. Moreover, the hydrogel film with 60 % by weight of CQDs demonstrates a tensile strength of 3.77 MPa and a strain to fracture of 83.2 %, which increases the softness of the film with the increase of CQDs, but it has less resistance than 50 % by weight of CQDs, which is probably due to the improper accumulation of nanoparticles in the polymer matrix. Based on the information obtained from the tensile test, the hydrogel film with 50 % by weight of CQDs exhibits good flexibility and resistance, making it suitable for use in various fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating the solubility of hydrogel granules in water (WS)
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 13
a illustrates the effect of adding different percentages of CQDs on the solubility value of soft heat hydrogel films of starch in water. The percentage of the total water-soluble material for the thermoplastic film of starch (glycerol and citric acid) is 15.6 %, which increases the solubility to 10.76, 14.6, and 21.21 with the increase in the concentration of CQDs for 40 %, 50 %, and 60 %, respectively. In other words, as the concentration of CQDs increases, the solubility percentage also increases. However, when comparing the film with 50 % by weight of CQDs with the film without CQDs (50 % by weight of glycerin and citric acid), the solubility in TPS-CQDs nanocomposite decreased. The reason for this could be the greater solubility of glycerol compared to CQDs. Glycerol reacts in aqueous medium by establishing hydrogen bonds with water, reducing the cohesion of the starch film network, and increasing its solubility.

Fig. 13 b depicts the solubility graph of fluorescent hydrogel granules with different weight ratios of chitosan and gelatin after 24 h of immersion in water. As observed in the diagram, the lowest solubility is associated with the hydrogel with a weight ratio (Ge 25 %: Ch 75 %), which can be attributed to cross connections and a strong bond between them. The highest solubility is observed in the weight ratio (Ge 50 %: Ch 50 %) due to their weak cross connections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating the swelling behavior of TPS hydrogel films over time
ce:section.ce:para:
The swelling ratio of the hydrogels samples was calculated using Equation (3):
(3)
S =
W t
-
W 0
W 0
× 100
where
W t
is the weight of the swollen gel in certain time interval, and
W 0
is the initial weight of hydrogels [65].

Fig. 14
a depicts the degree of swelling of thermoplastic hydrogel films of starch without CQDs (with glycerin and citric acid) and those containing CQDs with different concentrations. The graph shows that the swelling rate of hydrogel films increases over time. The swelling rate of the hydrogel film without CQDs is higher than that of the hydrogel films containing CQDs. This can be attributed to the presence of glycerin in the composition of the starch film without CQDs, which reacts with water by establishing hydrogen bonds and reduces the cohesion of the film network, allowing the starch to absorb more water. Additionally, as the concentration of CQDs in the polymer substrate increases, the swelling of the hydrogel decreases. These results indicate that CQDs nanoparticles have an effect on the polymer network, stiffening the network structure of the polymer substrate due to the increase in the percentage of CQDs.

Fig. 14 b illustrates the hydrogel before and after swelling. The difference between the granular hydrogel before and after swelling is the increase in size and volume of the hydrogel due to the absorption of water or other solvents. Swelling occurs when the hydrogel absorbs water, causing it to expand and increase in volume. This property is often utilized in applications such as drug delivery and tissue engineering.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different pH on swelling ratio
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 15 depicts the swelling behavior of fluorescent granular hydrogels containing CQDs in acidic, neutral, and alkaline environments with pH values of 3, 7, and 10, respectively. It highlights the highest amount of swelling of hydrogels at pH levels of 3, 7, and 10. The reason for this could be the conversion of amino groups on chitosan chains in an acidic environment to groups with a positive charge of ammonium, resulting in increased electrostatic repulsion between chitosan chains and enhanced water absorption ability [66]. In the comparison of the swelling of fluorescent granular hydrogels (Ch: Ge-CQDs) and (Ch-CQDs), the strong transverse connections and the reduction of holes and empty space between networks in the granular hydrogel (Ch: Ge-CQDs) lead to diminished water absorption and decreased inflation.

The equation (4) was used to calculate the WVP of the films:
(4)
WVP =
W × X
A × t × Δ P
Where
w
is the change in weight (g),
x
is the thickness of the film (cm),
A
is the area tested (cm2),
t
is time (s), and
Δ P
is the partial pressure difference of the water vapor across the film (Pa). The average of samples was determined for each type of film.

Fig. 16 represents the Water Vapor Permeability (WVP) in pure starch hydrogel film without CQDs and with varying percentages of CQDs. The WVP for starch film (prepared with glycerin and citric acid) is 1.66 × 10-7 g/mhPa, and it decreased to 1.64 × 10-7 g/mhPa for 40 % by weight of CQDs. However, it reached 1.53 × 10-7 g/mhPa for 50 % by weight of CQDs, indicating a relative decrease. For 60 % by weight of CQDs, it increased to 2.03 × 10-7 g/mhPa. The increase in CQDs led to an increase in water vapor permeability. Comparing the hydrogel film with 50 % by weight of CQDs with the starch film prepared with 50 % by weight of glycerin and citric acid, the permeability to water vapor in the CQDs nanocomposite decreased, with the best condition observed at 50 % wt of CQDs. The decrease in WVP by adding 50 % wt of CQDs compared to the pure starch film is attributed to the decrease in the solubility coefficient of CQDs compared to glycerol, impacting the effective moisture diffusion coefficient and solubility coefficient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions with fluorescent specimens
ce:section.ce:para: To identify Fe3+ ions selectively, a piece of the samples was immersed in different solutions for different time intervals and check the samples under the fluorescent UV lamp, which for Fe3+ ions the fluorescence intensity was quenched quickly after 20 min. The changes are shown in Fig. 17 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence spectrum of TPS-CQDs hydrogel films after contact with ions
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 18 displays the photoluminescence spectrum of TPS-CQDs fluorescent hydrogel films after immersion in different solutions for three ions: iron (II), iron (III), and mercury (II) at excitation wavelengths of 300 nm. It is evident that in the presence of ions, a shift in the emission wavelength has been observed, and the fluorescent intensity has decreased. The fluorescence of iron ion (III) is almost completely extinguished under the UV lamp, possibly due to electron transfers from excited state electrons of carbon quantum dots to the d orbital of iron (III). The fluorescent quenching of these films in the presence of iron (III) indicates the selective recognition of iron ions (III) by the synthesized samples [67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism and measurement of coloration
ce:section.ce:para: The colorimetric assay was effectively used to qualitatively and quantitatively determine iron (II), iron (III), and mercury (II) in water. The fluorescence that varies with excitation is believed to arise from aromatic conjugation structures, carbon excitons, surface states, emissive traps, and free zig-zag sites. The presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs can lead to the formation of intra-molecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonds, giving the CQDs distinct surface properties. When heavy metals interact with the fluorescent CQDs via the hydroxyl groups, stable complexes are formed, leading to changes in the physical and chemical characteristics of the fluorophores, such as fluorescence intensity and anisotropy. Furthermore, it offered a significant signal capable of discerning the analyte with great sensitivity [68]. As a consequence, the transition between fluorescent and non-fluorescent states was primarily initiated by the introduction of metal ions, as a result of non-radiative electron transfer from the excited state to the d-orbital of the metal ions. The creation of non-fluorescent chelating complexes between CQDs and Fe2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ results in a non-fluorescent “OFF” state [26]. The quenching mechanism is governed by the Stern-Volmer equation [69].

In conclusion, different hydrogel samples were obtained using CQDs. The incorporation of CQDs in TPS films resulted in transparent films with a constant quantum yield of 83%. UV–Vis spectra revealed transparency from λmax = 300 nm and above, indicating potential for optoelectronic applications. The CQDs, with sizes ranging from 1.57 nm to 1.64 nm and an average size of 1.59 nm, contributed to the film's properties. Additionally, the surface charge of the nanoparticles was measured at +25 mV after dissolving in distilled water. The decrease in melting temperature from 197 °C to 157 °C for TPS-CQDs samples can be attributed to the presence of citric acid, acting as a cross-linker, enhancing crystallinity and order within the films. The use of CQDs prepared with glycerin and citric acid as crosslinkers in nanocomposite samples further highlights the potential for tailored material properties. This study demonstrates the promising potential of TPS-CQDs nanocomposite films for various applications, including in optoelectronics and packaging.

Zeynab Hassanzadeh Baraz: Methodology, Investigation. Nasser Arsalani: Supervision, Resources. Samin Naghash-Hamed: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
